topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara scrolled through the listings, her thumb hovering over the screen, then darting back to the same address. The faded blue paint of the porch swing, the exact crooked placement of the overgrown lilac bush by the window—it all screamed *her* house. A slow, unsettling pressure began to build behind her ribs. She clicked on the listing. The photos were crisp, modern, almost sterile, yet the architecture was undeniably familiar. A strange tremor ran through her fingers as she read the price tag. It was listed for a figure that made her inhale sharply. She leaned back in her chair, the cheap fabric squeaking under her weight, and stared at the address as if it might spontaneously combust. Who? How? The air in the room suddenly felt thin, heavy with unspoken questions.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the screen, his jaw slack. He reread the property description three times, his eyes tracking over the meticulously detailed descriptions of the garden, the layout of the kitchen—every detail a ghost of his memory. He felt a peculiar lightness in his chest, a sudden, dizzying sense of dislocation. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room. He touched the cool glass of the windowpane, his knuckles white. "This can't be right," he murmured to the empty apartment. He walked to the door, his hand trembling as he reached for the handle, his mind racing through possibilities that felt too large, too impossible. The familiar scent of dust and old wood seemed to cling to the air around him, a phantom presence he couldn't quite place.

[story 3]

When Sarah saw the listing for Willow Creek Lane, she stopped breathing. Her breath hitched, shallow and rapid. She zoomed in on a picture of the old oak tree at the end of the driveway, a tree she hadn't seen in fifteen years. A coldness washed over her, creeping up her arms. She closed her laptop with a decisive snap, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet room. She stood up, walking to the window and pressing her forehead against the cool glass. The world outside seemed suddenly too sharp, too real, as if the very fabric of her known existence had just been subtly rearranged. She didn't move for a long time, just letting the silence of the sudden realization settle over her.
